(P3225) == * 4380005006158 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県いわき市平揚土30', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:子鍬倉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48755655}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kokuwakura Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/engisiki/mutu/bun/mut281207-01.html/ |title=Kokuwakura Shrine |date=20181009064716}} {{Shinto navbox}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === (P3225) == * 4380005006158 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県いわき市平揚土30', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:子鍬倉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48755655}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kokuwakura Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === (P3225) == * 4380005006158 == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県いわき市平揚土30', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:子鍬倉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q48755655}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kokuwakura Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|WD=Q719665|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|WD=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|WD=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|WD=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|WD=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|WD=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|WD=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|WD=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|WD=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|WD=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|WD=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|WD=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|WD=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|WD=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|WD=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|WD=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|WD=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|WD=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|WD=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|WD=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|WD=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|WD=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|WD=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|WD=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|WD=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/engisiki/mutu/bun/mut281207-01.html/ |title=Kokuwakura Shrine |date=20181009064716}} {{Shinto navbox}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kokuwakura Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kokuwakura Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Iwaki, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Iwaki]] [[Category:磐城平藩]]

link|Q48755655}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kokuwakura Shrine |Image = [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|qid=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}{{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|qid=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}} Agedo 30 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Inari Ōkami|Q719665|qid=Q719665|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Inari Ōkami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Established = First year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|qid=Q30021|1=806}}) |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = Kokuwakura Inari Shrine |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 18|Q2521|qid=Q2521|1=April 18}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Kokuwakura Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kokuwakura Jinja Iriguchi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Shrine grounds entrance}}]] '''Kokuwakura Shrine''' (こくわくらじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Agedo, {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|qid=Q128846894|lt=Taira|1=Taira}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki City|Q274384|qid=Q274384|1=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was designated a prefectural shrine. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|qid=Q30021|1=806}}). It is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} and one of the seven Shikinaisha of Iwaki Province. In ancient times it was said to be the general guardian shrine of the four districts of Iwaki ({{ill|lt=Kikuta District|Q11619613|qid=Q11619613|1=Kikuta district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwaki District|Q11587876|qid=Q11587876|1=Iwaki district}}, {{ill|lt=Iwamae District|Q11587858|qid=Q11587858|1=Iwasaki district}}, and {{ill|lt=Naraha District|Q11541347|qid=Q11541347|1=Naraha district}}). As one of the “Three Shrines of Taira Castle,” deities protecting {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Castle|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was venerated by successive lords of the castle. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}), construction of {{ill|lt=Iwaki-Taira Castle|Q11587869|qid=Q11587869|1=Iwakitaira Castle}} reduced the shrine’s land, leading to its decline. However, in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|qid=Q7004|1=1826}}), the fourth lord of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taira Domain|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Naitō Yoshitsuna|Q8514975|qid=Q8514975|1=Naitō Yoshimune}}, restored a small {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}} shrine that had stood in Sakuramachi and relocated it to the present site. In March of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was elevated to the rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Even after the abolition of the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 21, it continued to be affectionately called “Kensha” (prefectural shrine). In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 7, the {{ill|lt=cliff|Q1174791|qid=Q1174791|1=Escarpment}} on the southern front was cut away to create the “Men’s Slope,” and in {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 10 the “Women’s Slope” was completed. === Shrine Name === “{{ill|lt=Ko|Q1307938|qid=Q1307938|1=Ko}}” is said to mean “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=silkworm|1=UNKNOWN}},” symbolizing “clothing”; the agricultural tool “{{ill|lt=hoe|Q131154|qid=Q131154|1=Hoe (tool)}}” represents “food”; and “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kura (storehouse)|1=UNKNOWN}}” represents “shelter.” Together, the name is said to express the divine virtues of the enshrined deity governing “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=clothing, food, and shelter|1=UNKNOWN}}.” === Shrine Buildings === In April of {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 2, a fire destroyed the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shrine buildings|1=UNKNOWN}}, and most {{ill|lt=ancient documents|Q89298567|qid=Q89298567|1=komonjo}} and {{ill|lt=sacred treasures|Q11589458|qid=Q11589458|1=shinpō}} were lost. In May of {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|qid=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 6, the current main hall was constructed by master carpenter Yokota Mokusaemon of {{ill|lt=Takine Town|Q7678319|qid=Q7678319|1=Takine, Fukushima}}. Under the design and supervision of Tsukamoto Yoshitaka, a consultant to the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Education (Japan)|Q11080185|qid=Q11080185|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, the present {{ill|lt=Heiden|Q3076699|qid=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=Haiden|Q3125745|qid=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} were completed in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 4. === Auxiliary Shrines === * Akiba Shrine ** Enshrined deity: Kagutsuchi-no-Kami ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}) * Sugisha Tenmangū ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha3.jpg|Akiba Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha2.jpg|Tenmangū === Affiliated Shrine === * Yasaka Shrine File:Kokuwakura Jinja Keidaisha1.jpg|Yasaka Shrine === Access === * About a 10-minute walk west from “{{ill|lt=Iwaki Station|Q5353428|qid=Q5353428|1=Iwaki Station (Fukushima)}}” on the {{ill|lt=Jōban Line|Q1369263|qid=Q1369263|1=Jōban Line}} and {{ill|lt=Ban’etsu East Line|Q807037|qid=Q807037|1=Ban'etsu East Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|qid=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=Iino Hachimangū|Q11666702|qid=Q11666702|1=Iino Hachimangū}} * {{ill|lt=Taira (Iwaki City)|Q128846894|qid=Q128846894|1=Taira (Iwaki City)}} * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province|Q11658590|qid=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} === External Links === * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/engisiki/mutu/bun/mut281207-01.html/ |title=Kokuwakura Shrine |date=20181009064716}} {{Shinto navbox}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の旧県社]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Iwaki, Fukushima]] [[Category:History of Iwaki]] [[Category:磐城平藩]]
